title: Uniform electric fields
summary: Cambridge 9702 Paper 4 — Uniform electric fields (18.2). Senpai Corner diagram-backed pilot with premium structure and live visuals.
learningObjectives: Define and describe the characteristics of a uniform electric field.
Calculate electric field strength using force, charge, potential difference, and plate separation.
Explain and predict the motion of charged particles within uniform electric fields.

simpleExplanation.title: Electric Fields: Predictable & Powerful
simpleExplanation.summary: Imagine a special zone where the electric push or pull is exactly the same everywhere. That's a uniform electric field, vital for understanding how charged particles behave in devices.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of walking in a perfectly flat, horizontal room. No matter where you stand, gravity pulls you down with the exact same strength and direction. In a uniform electric field, a charged particle feels a constant electric force, just like you feel a constant gravitational force in that room.
steps: Draw straight, evenly spaced field lines.
Recall E = ΔV/d for field strength calculation.
Predict particle paths: straight or parabolic.

flashcards.front: What defines a uniform electric field?
flashcards.back: A region where the electric field strength (E) is constant in both magnitude and direction.

flashcards.front: How are uniform electric fields visually represented?
flashcards.back: By parallel, equally spaced electric field lines.

flashcards.front: What is the fundamental definition of electric field strength (E)?
flashcards.back: The force per unit positive test charge ($E = F/Q$).

flashcards.front: What is the formula for electric field strength (E) between parallel plates?
flashcards.back: $E = \frac{\Delta V}{d}$, where $\Delta V$ is potential difference and $d$ is plate separation.

flashcards.front: What are equipotential surfaces in a uniform electric field?
flashcards.back: Lines (or surfaces) parallel to the plates and perpendicular to the electric field lines, representing points of equal electric potential.

flashcards.front: How much work is done by the electric field when a charge moves along an equipotential line?
In which direction does a negative charge accelerate in a uniform electric field?
flashcards.back: Opposite to the direction of the electric field lines.

flashcards.front: Describe the path of a charged particle fired perpendicular to a uniform electric field.
flashcards.back: It will follow a parabolic trajectory.

flashcards.front: How is work done (W) related to charge (Q) and potential difference (ΔV) in an electric field?
What does the uniform density of electric field lines indicate?
flashcards.back: A constant electric field strength throughout the region.

sections.content: Dive into the fascinating world of **uniform electric fields**, where the electric push or pull is perfectly consistent! Unlike the variable fields around a single point charge, uniform fields offer a predictable environment, making them crucial for understanding how charged particles behave in devices like cathode ray tubes. Get ready to explore their characteristics, calculations, and the paths particles take within them. This pilot aligns with **Senpai Corner** A Level notes (2025–2027) for topic **18.2**.

A uniform electric field is a special region where the electric force on a charged particle is the same in both strength and direction, no matter where the particle is located within that region. This consistency is represented visually by straight, parallel, and equally spaced electric field lines.

items: E is now also defined by the units Vm -1.
The equation above can only be used for two charged parallel plates.
A charged particle will move through an electric field due to a force on it that is caused by said electric field.
The trajectory, as shown in the diagram above is parabolic .
The direction of parabola depends on the charged of the particle.

sections.content: At its core, electric field strength (E) quantifies the force exerted per unit of positive charge. This fundamental definition applies to all electric fields, uniform or not. When a charge 'q' is placed in a field 'E', it experiences a force 'F'.

The most common way to create a uniform electric field is by applying a potential difference ($\Delta V$) across two large, parallel metal plates separated by a distance ($d$). The field lines emerge perpendicularly from the positive plate and terminate perpendicularly on the negative plate.

This formula highlights that the electric field strength is also the magnitude of the potential gradient. It means for every metre you move across the field, the electric potential changes by 'E' Volts. The units for electric field strength can therefore be N C⁻¹ or V m⁻¹.

Imagine contour lines on a map showing points of equal height. Equipotential surfaces (or lines in 2D) are similar, connecting all points within the field that have the same electric potential. In a uniform field, these are simple.

items: Equipotential lines are parallel to the charged plates.
They are always perpendicular to the electric field lines.
No work is done by the electric field when a charge moves along an equipotential line.
Work is done when a charge (Q) moves between different equipotentials ($W = Q\Delta V$).

sections.content: When a charged particle enters a uniform electric field, it experiences a constant force, much like gravity on Earth. This constant force leads to constant acceleration, making its motion predictable using kinematic equations.

items: Positive charges accelerate in the same direction as the field lines.
Negative charges accelerate in the opposite direction to the field lines.
If fired parallel to the field, motion is linear.
If fired perpendicular to the field, the path is parabolic.

sections.question:
Two parallel plates are separated by 2.0 cm and have a potential difference of 500 V across them.
a) Calculate the electric field strength between the plates.
b) What force does an electron (charge = $-1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C) experience in this field?
sections.solution:
1.  Convert plate separation to metres: $d = 2.0 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$.
2.  Calculate electric field strength: $E = \frac{\Delta V}{d} = \frac{500 \text{ V}}{0.02 \text{ m}} = 25000 \text{ V m}^{-1}$ (or N C⁻¹).
3.  Calculate the magnitude of the force on the electron: $F = |Eq| = (25000 \text{ N C}^{-1}) \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}) = 4.0 \times 10^{-15} \text{ N}$.
4.  State the direction of the force: Since the electron is negative, the force is in the opposite direction to the electric field lines (i.e., towards the positive plate).

sections.content: Be careful with units! Electric field strength can be expressed as V m⁻¹ (Volts per metre) or N C⁻¹ (Newtons per Coulomb). Understand that these are equivalent and choose the most convenient for your calculation. Always state direction for vector quantities like force or electric field strength.

items.label: Senpai Corner A Level Physics notes (source)

faq.q: How do uniform electric fields differ from radial electric fields?
faq.a: Uniform fields have constant strength and direction, represented by parallel lines. Radial fields (from point charges) have strength that decreases with distance and lines that spread out from a central point.

faq.q: Why does a charged particle follow a parabolic path in a uniform electric field?
faq.a: When a particle enters perpendicular to the field, it experiences a constant electric force (and thus constant acceleration) in one direction, while maintaining constant velocity in the perpendicular direction. This combination of motions results in a parabolic trajectory, analogous to projectile motion under constant gravity.

faq.q: What is the significance of equipotential lines being perpendicular to electric field lines?
faq.a: This perpendicularity means that no component of the electric force acts along the equipotential line. Therefore, no work is done by the electric field when a charge moves along such a line, consistent with the definition of equal potential.
